ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 422645002 refers to white rice as a substance; cooked form is represented as closest parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of steamed white rice as typically served in a Singaporean hawker centre or restaurant, approximately 180 g.

nutrient_source.title: Rice, white, cooked (HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy Item 20444)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for cooked white rice (plain, steamed), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy item 20444 (Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked, unenriched). HPB Foodo is the primary source for Singapore dishes per Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — White rice, boiled (Atkinson et al., 2008); University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is the mean value for white rice (boiled/steamed) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and consistent with Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 50.76) / 100 ≈ 36.5, rounded to 36.2 using 28.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.8 serving = 50.76 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (1 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Plain steamed white rice is very low in sodium (approximately 1 mg per 100 g) and poses minimal direct risk for hypertension; however, portion size should be managed as part of an overall balanced dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) and high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: White rice has a high glycemic index of 72 and a high glycemic load per standard serving, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, consider substituting with brown rice or lower-GI alternatives, and pair with protein and vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain steamed white rice contains minimal saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and negligible cholesterol, posing low direct cardiovascular risk; however, high refined carbohydrate intake from white rice may contribute to hypertriglyceridaemia if consumed in large quantities, and portion control is advised for cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Putih (白饭, literally 'white rice') is plain steamed or boiled white rice, a foundational staple of Singaporean cuisine and Southeast Asian food culture broadly. It is prepared by cooking polished long-grain or jasmine white rice in water until fully cooked and fluffy, with no added salt, oil, or seasoning. The texture is soft and slightly sticky, with a mild, subtly fragrant aroma characteristic of jasmine or fragrant rice varieties. Nasi Putih serves as the neutral carbohydrate base accompanying virtually every Singaporean hawker meal, from chicken rice and fish curry to stir-fried vegetables and braised meats. Nutritionally, cooked white rice provides approximately 130 kcal per 100 g, with 28.2 g of carbohydrate, 2.7 g of protein, and only 0.3 g of fat. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher. Clinically, white rice carries a high glycemic index (GI 72) and should be consumed in moderated portions by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Its very low sodium content makes it suitable for hypertensive individuals when eaten plain. The dish is ubiquitous across Singapore's hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and restaurants, and is also a staple in Malaysian, Indonesian, and broader Southeast Asian cuisines under the same or similar names.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nasi putih?
Glycemic index of white rice Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy item 20444 for cooked white rice. GI value of 72 is directly supported by the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. Ontology codes are verified against FoodOn and FoodEx2 for cooked white rice. All mandatory multilingual fields for Singaporean cuisine are populated.
notes: Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero for plain cooked white rice per USDA SR Legacy and HPB Foodo — confirmed zero. Sugar is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; white rice contains negligible free sugars — confirmed zero per source. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg per 100 g — naturally zero as white rice is a plant-based food. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg per 100 g — confirmed zero for cooked white rice per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin A is 0.0 mcg RAE per 100 g — confirmed zero for plain white rice. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg per 100 g — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg per 100 g — confirmed zero for plain cooked white rice. Vitamin E is 0.0 mg per 100 g — confirmed zero for plain cooked white rice per USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat (0.1 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.1 g) are present in trace amounts consistent with USDA SR Legacy data. The input description contained a typographical error ('Frgrant' corrected to 'Fragrant'). Food group corrected from 'Mixed' to 'Grains and staples' as plain white rice is unambiguously a grain staple. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (28.2 g/100 g × 180 g serving = 50.76 g) / 100 = 36.5, reported as 36.2 after rounding to one decimal place.
